{{Shrine |name= Wakamiya Shrine |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} |image= [[File:Wakamiya-jinja.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡}} {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町}} Takihara 872 |latitude_deg = 34 | latitude_min = 21 | latitude_sec = 58.6 |longitude_deg = 136 | longitude_min = 25 | longitude_sec = 35 |map= Japan Mie |deity= Wakamiya no Kami |shrine_rank= {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}} subordinate shrine |established= Before {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228年}}) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |other_name= Ama-Wakamiya |pilgrimage= |annual_festival= |rituals= }}
'''Wakamiya Shrine''' (若宮神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, an associated shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.
The subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya include this shrine, {{ill|Nagayuke Shrine|ja|長由介神社}}, and {{ill|Kawashima Shrine|ja|川島神社}}, all located within the grounds of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="om6">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 686</ref>
== Overview ==
Located within the precincts of Takihara-no-Miya in {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町}}, {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 17</ref> It stands on a raised area to the right (east side<ref name="om6"/>) of the main shrine of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 86</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 73</ref> The customary order of visiting shrines within Takihara-no-Miya precincts is Takihara-no-Miya, Takihara Namimiya, Wakamiya Shrine, Nagayuke Shrine, and Kawashima Shrine.<ref name="gk"/>
Wakamiya Shrine holds the highest rank among the three subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="uy"/> It is also known as Ama-Wakamiya.<ref name="om6"/> The shrine faces south. According to the "Jingū Yōkō," the main hall is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, surrounded by a single-layered {{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣}} fence and gate, and features a {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}.<ref name="om7">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 687</ref>
The enshrined deity is Wakamiya no Kami.<ref name="uy"/><ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> It is considered a {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} connected to Takihara.<ref name="gk"/> Another theory identifies the deity as {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|Mikumari no Kami=Mikumari no Kami|lt=Mikumari no Kami}}.<ref name="ic"/> The "Ōmiya Town History" states that the deity is unknown, but an annotation in the Jingū Administration Office's publication "O-Ise Mairi" refers to it as "Wakamiya no Kami."<ref name="om">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): pp. 686–687</ref>
== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228年}}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192年}}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464年}}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662年}}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889年}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874年}} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>

revision.text:
{{Shrine |name= Wakamiya Shrine |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} |image= [[File:Wakamiya-jinja.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡}} {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町}} Takihara 872 |latitude_deg = 34 | latitude_min = 21 | latitude_sec = 58.6 |longitude_deg = 136 | longitude_min = 25 | longitude_sec = 35 |map= Japan Mie |deity= Wakamiya no Kami |shrine_rank= {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}} subordinate shrine |established= Before {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228年}}) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |other_name= Ama-Wakamiya |pilgrimage= |annual_festival= |rituals= }}
'''Wakamiya Shrine''' (若宮神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, an associated shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.
The subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya include this shrine, {{ill|Nagayuke Shrine|ja|長由介神社}}, and {{ill|Kawashima Shrine|ja|川島神社}}, all located within the grounds of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="om6">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 686</ref>
== Overview ==
Located within the precincts of Takihara-no-Miya in {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町}}, {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 17</ref> It stands on a raised area to the right (east side<ref name="om6"/>) of the main shrine of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 86</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 73</ref> The customary order of visiting shrines within Takihara-no-Miya precincts is Takihara-no-Miya, Takihara Namimiya, Wakamiya Shrine, Nagayuke Shrine, and Kawashima Shrine.<ref name="gk"/>
Wakamiya Shrine holds the highest rank among the three subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="uy"/> It is also known as Ama-Wakamiya.<ref name="om6"/> The shrine faces south. According to the "Jingū Yōkō," the main hall is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, surrounded by a single-layered {{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣}} fence and gate, and features a {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}.<ref name="om7">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 687</ref>
The enshrined deity is Wakamiya no Kami.<ref name="uy"/><ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> It is considered a {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} connected to Takihara.<ref name="gk"/> Another theory identifies the deity as {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|Mikumari no Kami=Mikumari no Kami|lt=Mikumari no Kami}}.<ref name="ic"/> The "Ōmiya Town History" states that the deity is unknown, but an annotation in the Jingū Administration Office's publication "O-Ise Mairi" refers to it as "Wakamiya no Kami."<ref name="om">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): pp. 686–687</ref>
== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228年}}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192年}}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464年}}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662年}}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889年}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874年}} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/> [[ja:若宮神社 (大紀町)]] == References ==
* Ise Bunka-sha (2008), ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Tour,'' Special Issue of ''Isejin,'' Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (1929), ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1,'' Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987), ''History of Ōmiya Town: Historical Edition,'' Ōmiya Town, March 31, 1987, 1133 pages. * Gakken Publishing (2013), ''Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine,'' Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection Vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011), ''Good Luck! Girls’♡ O-Ise Pilgrimage,'' Shufunotomo Co., Ltd., January 20, 2011, 95 pages. ISBN 978-4-07-276191-5== See Also ==

Wakamiya Shrine holds the highest rank among the three subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="uy"/> It is also known as Ama-Wakamiya.<ref name="om6"/> The shrine faces south. According to the "Jingū Yōkō," the main hall is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, surrounded by a single-layered {{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣}} fence and gate, and features a {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}.<ref name="om7">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 687</ref>
The enshrined deity is Wakamiya no Kami.<ref name="uy"/><ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> It is considered a {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} connected to Takihara.<ref name="gk"/> Another theory identifies the deity as {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|Mikumari no Kami=Mikumari no Kami|lt=Mikumari no Kami}}.<ref name="ic"/> The "Ōmiya Town History" states that the deity is unknown, but an annotation in the Jingū Administration Office's publication "O-Ise Mairi" refers to it as "Wakamiya no Kami."<ref name="om">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): pp. 686–687</ref>
== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228年}}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192年}}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464年}}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662年}}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889年}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874年}} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/> [[ja:若宮神社 (大紀町)]] == References ==
* Ise Bunka-sha (2008), ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Tour,'' Special Issue of ''Isejin,'' Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (1929), ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1,'' Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987), ''History of Ōmiya Town: Historical Edition,'' Ōmiya Town, March 31, 1987, 1133 pages. * Gakken Publishing (2013), ''Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine,'' Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection Vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011), ''Good Luck! Girls’♡ O-Ise Pilgrimage,'' Shufunotomo Co., Ltd., January 20, 2011, 95 pages. ISBN 978-4-07-276191-5 == See Also! ==

{{Shrine |name= Wakamiya Shrine |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} |image= [[File:Wakamiya-jinja.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡}} {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町}} Takihara 872 |latitude_deg = 34 | latitude_min = 21 | latitude_sec = 58.6 |longitude_deg = 136 | longitude_min = 25 | longitude_sec = 35 |map= Japan Mie |deity= Wakamiya no Kami |shrine_rank= {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}} subordinate shrine |established= Before {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228年}}) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |other_name= Ama-Wakamiya |pilgrimage= |annual_festival= |rituals= }}
'''Wakamiya Shrine''' (若宮神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, an associated shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.
The subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya include this shrine, {{ill|Nagayuke Shrine|ja|長由介神社}}, and {{ill|Kawashima Shrine|ja|川島神社}}, all located within the grounds of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="om6">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 686</ref>
== Overview ==
Located within the precincts of Takihara-no-Miya in {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町}}, {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 17</ref> It stands on a raised area to the right (east side<ref name="om6"/>) of the main shrine of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 86</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 73</ref> The customary order of visiting shrines within Takihara-no-Miya precincts is Takihara-no-Miya, Takihara Namimiya, Wakamiya Shrine, Nagayuke Shrine, and Kawashima Shrine.<ref name="gk"/>
Wakamiya Shrine holds the highest rank among the three subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="uy"/> It is also known as Ama-Wakamiya.<ref name="om6"/> The shrine faces south. According to the "Jingū Yōkō," the main hall is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, surrounded by a single-layered {{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣}} fence and gate, and features a {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}.<ref name="om7">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 687</ref>
The enshrined deity is Wakamiya no Kami.<ref name="uy"/><ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> It is considered a {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} connected to Takihara.<ref name="gk"/> Another theory identifies the deity as {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|Mikumari no Kami=Mikumari no Kami|lt=Mikumari no Kami}}.<ref name="ic"/> The "Ōmiya Town History" states that the deity is unknown, but an annotation in the Jingū Administration Office's publication "O-Ise Mairi" refers to it as "Wakamiya no Kami."<ref name="om">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): pp. 686–687</ref>
== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228年}}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192年}}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464年}}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662年}}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889年}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874年}} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/> [[ja:若宮神社 (大紀町)]] == References ==
* Ise Bunka-sha (2008), ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Tour,'' Special Issue of ''Isejin,'' Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (1929), ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1,'' Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987), ''History of Ōmiya Town: Historical Edition,'' Ōmiya Town, March 31, 1987, 1133 pages. * Gakken Publishing (2013), ''Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine,'' Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection Vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011), ''Good Luck! Girls’♡ O-Ise Pilgrimage,'' Shufunotomo Co., Ltd., January 20, 2011, 95 pages. ISBN 978-4-07-276191-5 == See Also ==

{{Shrine |name= Wakamiya Shrine |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} |image= [[File:Wakamiya-jinja.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡}} {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町}} Takihara 872 |latitude_deg = 34 | latitude_min = 21 | latitude_sec = 58.6 |longitude_deg = 136 | longitude_min = 25 | longitude_sec = 35 |map= Japan Mie |deity= Wakamiya no Kami |shrine_rank= {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}} subordinate shrine |established= Before {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228年}}) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |other_name= Ama-Wakamiya |pilgrimage= |annual_festival= |rituals= }}
'''Wakamiya Shrine''' (若宮神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, an associated shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.
The subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya include this shrine, {{ill|Nagayuke Shrine|ja|長由介神社}}, and {{ill|Kawashima Shrine|ja|川島神社}}, all located within the grounds of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="om6">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 686</ref>
== Overview ==
Located within the precincts of Takihara-no-Miya in {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町}}, {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 17</ref> It stands on a raised area to the right (east side<ref name="om6"/>) of the main shrine of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 86</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 73</ref> The customary order of visiting shrines within Takihara-no-Miya precincts is Takihara-no-Miya, Takihara Namimiya, Wakamiya Shrine, Nagayuke Shrine, and Kawashima Shrine.<ref name="gk"/>
Wakamiya Shrine holds the highest rank among the three subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="uy"/> It is also known as Ama-Wakamiya.<ref name="om6"/> The shrine faces south. According to the "Jingū Yōkō," the main hall is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, surrounded by a single-layered {{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣}} fence and gate, and features a {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}.<ref name="om7">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 687</ref>
The enshrined deity is Wakamiya no Kami.<ref name="uy"/><ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> It is considered a {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} connected to Takihara.<ref name="gk"/> Another theory identifies the deity as {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|Mikumari no Kami=Mikumari no Kami|lt=Mikumari no Kami}}.<ref name="ic"/> The "Ōmiya Town History" states that the deity is unknown, but an annotation in the Jingū Administration Office's publication "O-Ise Mairi" refers to it as "Wakamiya no Kami."<ref name="om">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): pp. 686–687</ref>
== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228年}}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192nd year }}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464年}}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662nd year }}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889年}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874年}} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014年}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/> [[ja:若宮神社 (大紀町)]] == References ==
* Ise Bunka-sha (2008), ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Tour,'' Special Issue of ''Isejin,'' Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (1929), ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1,'' Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987), ''History of Ōmiya Town: Historical Edition,'' Ōmiya Town, March 31, 1987, 1133 pages. * Gakken Publishing (2013), ''Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine,'' Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection Vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011), ''Good Luck! Girls’♡ O-Ise Pilgrimage,'' Shufunotomo Co., Ltd., January 20, 2011, 95 pages. ISBN 978-4-07-276191-5 == See Also ==

According to the "Jingū Yōkō," the main hall is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, surrounded by a single-layered {{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣}} fence and gate, and features a {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}.<ref name="om7">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 687</ref>
The enshrined deity is Wakamiya no Kami.<ref name="uy"/><ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> It is considered a {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} connected to Takihara.<ref name="gk"/> Another theory identifies the deity as {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|Mikumari no Kami=Mikumari no Kami|lt=Mikumari no Kami}}.<ref name="ic"/> The "Ōmiya Town History" states that the deity is unknown, but an annotation in the Jingū Administration Office's publication "O-Ise Mairi" refers to it as "Wakamiya no Kami."<ref name="om">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): pp. 686–687</ref>
== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year }}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192nd year }}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464th year }}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662nd year }}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889年}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874th year }} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year }} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909年}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/> [[ja:若宮神社 (大紀町)]] == References ==

The subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya include this shrine, {{ill|Nagayuke Shrine|ja|長由介神社}}, and {{ill|Kawashima Shrine|ja|川島神社}}, all located within the grounds of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="om6">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 686</ref>

Located within the precincts of Takihara-no-Miya in {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町}}, {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 17</ref> It stands on a raised area to the right (east side<ref name="om6"/>) of the main shrine of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 86</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 73</ref> The customary order of visiting shrines within Takihara-no-Miya precincts is Takihara-no-Miya, Takihara Namimiya, Wakamiya Shrine, Nagayuke Shrine, and Kawashima Shrine.<ref name="gk"/>

Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year }} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909th year }} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>

{{Shrine |name= Wakamiya Shrine |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} |image= [[File:Wakamiya-jinja.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡}} {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町}} Takihara 872 |latitude_deg = 34 | latitude_min = 21 | latitude_sec = 58.6 |longitude_deg = 136 | longitude_min = 25 | longitude_sec = 35 |map= Japan Mie |deity= Wakamiya no Kami |shrine_rank= {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}} subordinate shrine |established= Before {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year }}) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |other_name= Ama-Wakamiya |pilgrimage= |annual_festival= |rituals= }}
'''Wakamiya Shrine''' (若宮神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮}}, an associated shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.
The subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya include this shrine, {{ill|Nagayuke Shrine|ja|長由介神社}}, and {{ill|Kawashima Shrine|ja|川島神社}}, all located within the grounds of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="om6">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 686</ref>
== Overview ==
Located within the precincts of Takihara-no-Miya in {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町}}, {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 17</ref> It stands on a raised area to the right (east side<ref name="om6"/>) of the main shrine of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 86</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 73</ref> The customary order of visiting shrines within Takihara-no-Miya precincts is Takihara-no-Miya, Takihara Namimiya, Wakamiya Shrine, Nagayuke Shrine, and Kawashima Shrine.<ref name="gk"/>
Wakamiya Shrine holds the highest rank among the three subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="uy"/> It is also known as Ama-Wakamiya.<ref name="om6"/> The shrine faces south. According to the "Jingū Yōkō," the main hall is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, surrounded by a single-layered {{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣}} fence and gate, and features a {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居}}.<ref name="om7">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 687</ref>
The enshrined deity is Wakamiya no Kami.<ref name="uy"/><ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> It is considered a {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} connected to Takihara.<ref name="gk"/> Another theory identifies the deity as {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|Mikumari no Kami=Mikumari no Kami|lt=Mikumari no Kami}}.<ref name="ic"/> The "Ōmiya Town History" states that the deity is unknown, but an annotation in the Jingū Administration Office's publication "O-Ise Mairi" refers to it as "Wakamiya no Kami."<ref name="om">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): pp. 686–687</ref>
== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year }}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192nd year }}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464th year }}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662nd year }}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889th year }} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874th year }} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year }} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909th year }} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/> [[ja:若宮神社 (大紀町)]] == References ==
* Ise Bunka-sha (2008), ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Tour,'' Special Issue of ''Isejin,'' Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (1929), ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1,'' Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987), ''History of Ōmiya Town: Historical Edition,'' Ōmiya Town, March 31, 1987, 1133 pages. * Gakken Publishing (2013), ''Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine,'' Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection Vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011), ''Good Luck! Girls’♡ O-Ise Pilgrimage,'' Shufunotomo Co., Ltd., January 20, 2011, 95 pages. ISBN 978-4-07-276191-5 == See Also ==

== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year|12=draft}}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|12=draft}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192nd year|12=draft}}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正|12=draft}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464th year|12=draft}}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662nd year|12=draft}}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|12=draft}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道|12=draft}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線|12=draft}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道|12=draft}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/>
== References ==

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (1929), ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1,'' Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987), ''History of Ōmiya Town: Historical Edition,'' Ōmiya Town, March 31, 1987, 1133 pages. * Gakken Publishing (2013), ''Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine,'' Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection Vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|12=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011), ''Good Luck! Girls’♡ O-Ise Pilgrimage,'' Shufunotomo Co., Ltd., January 20, 2011, 95 pages. ISBN 978-4-07-276191-5 == See Also ==

{{Shrine |name= Wakamiya Shrine |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} |image= [[File:Wakamiya-jinja.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡|12=draft}} {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町|12=draft}} Takihara 872 |latitude_deg = 34 | latitude_min = 21 | latitude_sec = 58.6 |longitude_deg = 136 | longitude_min = 25 | longitude_sec = 35 |map= Japan Mie |deity= Wakamiya no Kami |shrine_rank= {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=draft}} subordinate shrine |established= Before {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year|12=draft}}) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} |other_name= Ama-Wakamiya |pilgrimage= |annual_festival= |rituals= }}
'''Wakamiya Shrine''' (若宮神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=draft}}, an associated shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} (Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}.
The subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya include this shrine, {{ill|Nagayuke Shrine|ja|長由介神社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kawashima Shrine|ja|川島神社|12=draft}}, all located within the grounds of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="om6">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 686</ref>
== Overview ==
Located within the precincts of Takihara-no-Miya in {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 17</ref> It stands on a raised area to the right (east side<ref name="om6"/>) of the main shrine of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 86</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 73</ref> The customary order of visiting shrines within Takihara-no-Miya precincts is Takihara-no-Miya, Takihara Namimiya, Wakamiya Shrine, Nagayuke Shrine, and Kawashima Shrine.<ref name="gk"/>
Wakamiya Shrine holds the highest rank among the three subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="uy"/> It is also known as Ama-Wakamiya.<ref name="om6"/> The shrine faces south. According to the "Jingū Yōkō," the main hall is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} style with a thatched roof, surrounded by a single-layered {{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣|12=draft}} fence and gate, and features a {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居|12=draft}}.<ref name="om7">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 687</ref>
The enshrined deity is Wakamiya no Kami.<ref name="uy"/><ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> It is considered a {{ill|water deity|ja|水神|12=draft}} connected to Takihara.<ref name="gk"/> Another theory identifies the deity as {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|Mikumari no Kami=Mikumari no Kami|lt=Mikumari no Kami|12=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/> The "Ōmiya Town History" states that the deity is unknown, but an annotation in the Jingū Administration Office's publication "O-Ise Mairi" refers to it as "Wakamiya no Kami."<ref name="om">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): pp. 686–687</ref>
== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year|12=draft}}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|12=draft}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192nd year|12=draft}}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正|12=draft}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464th year|12=draft}}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662nd year|12=draft}}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|12=draft}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>

{{Shrine |name= Wakamiya Shrine |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} |image= [[File:Wakamiya-jinja.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡|12=draft}} {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町|12=draft}} Takihara 872 |latitude_deg = 34 | latitude_min = 21 | latitude_sec = 58.6 |longitude_deg = 136 | longitude_min = 25 | longitude_sec = 35 |map= Japan Mie |deity= Wakamiya no Kami |shrine_rank= {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=draft}} subordinate shrine |established= Before {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year|12=draft}}) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} |other_name= Ama-Wakamiya |pilgrimage= |annual_festival= |rituals= }}
'''Wakamiya Shrine''' (若宮神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=draft}}, an associated shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} (Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}.
The subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya include this shrine, {{ill|Nagayuke Shrine|ja|長由介神社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kawashima Shrine|ja|川島神社|12=draft}}, all located within the grounds of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="om6">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 686</ref>
== Overview ==
Located within the precincts of Takihara-no-Miya in {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 17</ref> It stands on a raised area to the right (east side<ref name="om6"/>) of the main shrine of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 86</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 73</ref> The customary order of visiting shrines within Takihara-no-Miya precincts is Takihara-no-Miya, Takihara Namimiya, Wakamiya Shrine, Nagayuke Shrine, and Kawashima Shrine.<ref name="gk"/>
Wakamiya Shrine holds the highest rank among the three subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="uy"/> It is also known as Ama-Wakamiya.<ref name="om6"/> The shrine faces south. According to the "Jingū Yōkō," the main hall is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} style with a thatched roof, surrounded by a single-layered {{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣|12=draft}} fence and gate, and features a {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居|12=draft}}.<ref name="om7">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 687</ref>
The enshrined deity is Wakamiya no Kami.<ref name="uy"/><ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> It is considered a {{ill|water deity|ja|水神|12=draft}} connected to Takihara.<ref name="gk"/> Another theory identifies the deity as {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|Mikumari no Kami=Mikumari no Kami|lt=Mikumari no Kami|12=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/> The "Ōmiya Town History" states that the deity is unknown, but an annotation in the Jingū Administration Office's publication "O-Ise Mairi" refers to it as "Wakamiya no Kami."<ref name="om">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): pp. 686–687</ref>
== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year|12=draft}}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|12=draft}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192nd year|12=draft}}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正|12=draft}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464th year|12=draft}}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662nd year|12=draft}}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|12=draft}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道|12=draft}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線|12=draft}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道|12=draft}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/>
== References ==
* Ise Bunka-sha (2008), ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Tour,'' Special Issue of ''Isejin,'' Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (1929), ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1,'' Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987), ''History of Ōmiya Town: Historical Edition,'' Ōmiya Town, March 31, 1987, 1133 pages. * Gakken Publishing (2013), ''Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine,'' Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection Vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|12=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011), ''Good Luck! Girls’♡ O-Ise Pilgrimage,'' Shufunotomo Co., Ltd., January 20, 2011, 95 pages. ISBN 978-4-07-276191-5 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft}}

Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|12=draft}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道|12=draft}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線|12=draft}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道|12=draft}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/>
== References ==
* Ise Bunka-sha (2008), ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Tour,'' Special Issue of ''Isejin,'' Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (1929), ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1,'' Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987), ''History of Ōmiya Town: Historical Edition,'' Ōmiya Town, March 31, 1987, 1133 pages. * Gakken Publishing (2013), ''Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine,'' Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection Vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|12=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011), ''Good Luck! Girls’♡ O-Ise Pilgrimage,'' Shufunotomo Co., Ltd., January 20, 2011, 95 pages. ISBN 978-4-07-276191-5 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft}}
== References == <references /> == External Links == {{external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [https://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area01/area01_04.jpg 若宮神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m13/1304.html 若宮神社（わかみやじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

{{Shrine |name= Wakamiya Shrine |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} |image= [[File:Wakamiya-jinja.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡|12=draft}} {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町|12=draft}} Takihara 872 |latitude_deg = 34 | latitude_min = 21 | latitude_sec = 58.6 |longitude_deg = 136 | longitude_min = 25 | longitude_sec = 35 |map= Japan Mie |deity= Wakamiya no Kami |shrine_rank= {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=draft}} subordinate shrine |established= Before {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year|12=draft}}) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} |other_name= Ama-Wakamiya |pilgrimage= |annual_festival= |rituals= }}
'''Wakamiya Shrine''' (若宮神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=draft}}, an associated shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} (Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}.
The subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya include this shrine, {{ill|Nagayuke Shrine|ja|長由介神社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kawashima Shrine|ja|川島神社|12=draft}}, all located within the grounds of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="om6">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 686</ref>
== Overview ==
Located within the precincts of Takihara-no-Miya in {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 17</ref> It stands on a raised area to the right (east side<ref name="om6"/>) of the main shrine of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 86</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 73</ref> The customary order of visiting shrines within Takihara-no-Miya precincts is Takihara-no-Miya, Takihara Namimiya, Wakamiya Shrine, Nagayuke Shrine, and Kawashima Shrine.<ref name="gk"/>
Wakamiya Shrine holds the highest rank among the three subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="uy"/> It is also known as Ama-Wakamiya.<ref name="om6"/> The shrine faces south. According to the "Jingū Yōkō," the main hall is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} style with a thatched roof, surrounded by a single-layered {{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣|12=draft}} fence and gate, and features a {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居|12=draft}}.<ref name="om7">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 687</ref>
The enshrined deity is Wakamiya no Kami.<ref name="uy"/><ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> It is considered a {{ill|water deity|ja|水神|12=draft}} connected to Takihara.<ref name="gk"/> Another theory identifies the deity as {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|Mikumari no Kami=Mikumari no Kami|lt=Mikumari no Kami|12=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/> The "Ōmiya Town History" states that the deity is unknown, but an annotation in the Jingū Administration Office's publication "O-Ise Mairi" refers to it as "Wakamiya no Kami."<ref name="om">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): pp. 686–687</ref>
== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year|12=draft}}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|12=draft}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192nd year|12=draft}}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正|12=draft}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464th year|12=draft}}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662nd year|12=draft}}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|12=draft}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道|12=draft}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線|12=draft}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道|12=draft}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/>
== References ==
* Ise Bunka-sha (2008), ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Tour,'' Special Issue of ''Isejin,'' Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (1929), ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1,'' Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987), ''History of Ōmiya Town: Historical Edition,'' Ōmiya Town, March 31, 1987, 1133 pages. * Gakken Publishing (2013), ''Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine,'' Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection Vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|12=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011), ''Good Luck! Girls’♡ O-Ise Pilgrimage,'' Shufunotomo Co., Ltd., January 20, 2011, 95 pages. {{Shrine |name= Wakamiya Shrine |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}} |image= [[File:Wakamiya-jinja.jpg|270px]] |location= {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町|12=draft|12=draft}} Takihara 872 |latitude_deg = 34 | latitude_min = 21 | latitude_sec = 58.6 |longitude_deg = 136 | longitude_min = 25 | longitude_sec = 35 |map= Japan Mie |deity= Wakamiya no Kami |shrine_rank= {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=draft|12=draft}} subordinate shrine |established= Before {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year|12=draft|12=draft}}) |main_hall= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} |other_name= Ama-Wakamiya |pilgrimage= |annual_festival= |rituals= }}
'''Wakamiya Shrine''' (若宮神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, an associated shrine of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (Inner Shrine) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya include this shrine, {{ill|Nagayuke Shrine|ja|長由介神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kawashima Shrine|ja|川島神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, all located within the grounds of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="om6">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 686</ref>
== Overview ==
Located within the precincts of Takihara-no-Miya in {{ill|Taiki, Mie|ja|大紀町|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Watarai District|ja|度会郡|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 17</ref> It stands on a raised area to the right (east side<ref name="om6"/>) of the main shrine of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 86</ref><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 73</ref> The customary order of visiting shrines within Takihara-no-Miya precincts is Takihara-no-Miya, Takihara Namimiya, Wakamiya Shrine, Nagayuke Shrine, and Kawashima Shrine.<ref name="gk"/>
Wakamiya Shrine holds the highest rank among the three subordinate shrines of Takihara-no-Miya.<ref name="uy"/> It is also known as Ama-Wakamiya.<ref name="om6"/> The shrine faces south. According to the "Jingū Yōkō," the main hall is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft|12=draft}} style with a thatched roof, surrounded by a single-layered {{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣|12=draft|12=draft}} fence and gate, and features a {{ill|Shinmei torii|ja|神明鳥居|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="om7">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): p. 687</ref>
The enshrined deity is Wakamiya no Kami.<ref name="uy"/><ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/> It is considered a {{ill|water deity|ja|水神|1=Suijin|lt=water god|12=draft|12=draft}} connected to Takihara.<ref name="gk"/> Another theory identifies the deity as {{ill|Mikumari|ja|水分神|Mikumari no Kami=Mikumari no Kami|lt=Mikumari no Kami|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/> The "Ōmiya Town History" states that the deity is unknown, but an annotation in the Jingū Administration Office's publication "O-Ise Mairi" refers to it as "Wakamiya no Kami."<ref name="om">Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987): pp. 686–687</ref>
== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year|12=draft|12=draft}}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192nd year|12=draft|12=draft}}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464th year|12=draft|12=draft}}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662nd year|12=draft|12=draft}}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft|12=draft}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線|12=draft|12=draft}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/>
== References ==
* Ise Bunka-sha (2008), ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Tour,'' Special Issue of ''Isejin,'' Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (1929), ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1,'' Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987), ''History of Ōmiya Town: Historical Edition,'' Ōmiya Town, March 31, 1987, 1133 pages. * Gakken Publishing (2013), ''Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine,'' Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection Vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|12=draft|12=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011), ''Good Luck! Girls’♡ O-Ise Pilgrimage,'' Shufunotomo Co., Ltd., January 20, 2011, 95 pages. ISBN 978-4-07-276191-5 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}}
== References == <references /> == External Links == {{external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [https://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area01/area01_04.jpg 若宮神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m13/1304.html 若宮神社（わかみやじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

== History ==
[[File:Takihara-no-miya 15.JPG|thumb|Shrine buildings before the relocation in November 2014. The left structure houses the sacred boat (御船倉), said to have been used by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>]]
The founding date is unknown.<ref name="om"/> The earliest known mention of Wakamiya Shrine is in the "Naikū Sengu-ki" from {{ill|Antei|ja|安貞|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1228|ja|1228th year|12=draft|12=draft}}).<ref name="om6"/> A reference exists in the "Annual Events of the Imperial Grand Shrine" from {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|12=draft|12=draft}} 3 ({{ill|1192|ja|1192nd year|12=draft|12=draft}}), noting visits to Namimiya, Kawashima, Nagayuke, and Ama-Wakamiya Shrines after the main rituals, but this was later annotated by Arakida Moritsune in {{ill|Kanshō|ja|寛正|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 ({{ill|1464|ja|1464th year|12=draft|12=draft}}).<ref name="om"/>
Since {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 ({{ill|1662|ja|1662nd year|12=draft|12=draft}}), Wakamiya Shrine and Nagayuke Shrine have been included in the scope of {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Edo Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="om7"/> From {{ill|1889|ja|1889th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (Meiji 22), they have been maintained by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="om7"/> Nagayuke Shrine was originally south-facing like Wakamiya Shrine but was reoriented westward in {{ill|1874|ja|1874th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (Meiji 7), with new enclosures and gates added.<ref name="om7"/> At that time, Kawashima Shrine was merged with Nagayuke Shrine.<ref name="om7"/>
Wakamiya Shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|2014|ja|2014th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (Heisei 26), with the previous relocation occurring in {{ill|1909|ja|1909th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (Meiji 42).<ref name="uy"/>
== Access ==
* Approximately a 36-minute walk (about 1.9 km) from {{ill|Takihara Station|ja|滝原駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft|12=draft}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 83</ref> Limited train service, with gaps of 2–3 hours between trains, leading some travel guides to recommend using a car.<ref>Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011): p. 44</ref> * Directly accessible from the "Takihara-no-Miya-mae" bus stop on the {{ill|Matsusaka-Kumano Line|ja|松阪熊野線|12=draft|12=draft}} (formerly Nanki Express Bus) operated by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Mie Tourism Federation|url=http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2944.html|title=皇大神宮別宮 瀧原宮|work=Visit Mie|accessdate=2021-02-06}}</ref> * About 10 minutes (300 m) south from {{ill|Ōmiya-Ōdai IC|ja|大宮大台インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kisei Expressway|ja|紀勢自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}}, via {{ill|National Route 42 (Japan)|ja|国道42号|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="mkr"/> Parking available for 100 vehicles.<ref name="mkr"/>
== References ==
* Ise Bunka-sha (2008), ''O-Ise-san 125 Shrine Tour,'' Special Issue of ''Isejin,'' Ise Bunka-sha, December 23, 2008, 151 pages. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * Ujiyamada City Office (1929), ''History of Ujiyamada City, Volume 1,'' Ujiyamada City Office, January 20, 1929, 862 pages. * Ōmiya Town History Compilation Committee (1987), ''History of Ōmiya Town: Historical Edition,'' Ōmiya Town, March 31, 1987, 1133 pages. * Gakken Publishing (2013), ''Let’s Go to Ise Grand Shrine,'' Gakken Mook Shrine Travel Selection Vol.1, supervised by {{ill|Minoru Sonoda|ja|薗田稔|12=draft|12=draft}}, Gakken Marketing, July 4, 2013, 82 pages. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * Shufunotomo Co., Ltd. (2011), ''Good Luck! Girls’♡ O-Ise Pilgrimage,'' Shufunotomo Co., Ltd., January 20, 2011, 95 pages. ISBN 978-4-07-276191-5 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}}
== References == <references /> == External Links == {{external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [https://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area01/area01_04.jpg 若宮神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m13/1304.html 若宮神社（わかみやじんじゃ）] - 財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会
